prompt: We added a `Today is {iso_timestamp}` line to the top of our system prompt for debugging. Cache hit rate dropped to 0%. We're on Anthropic. What happened?
rubric_notes: Textbook cache-key-poisoning. Should explain that any dynamic value above the cache boundary changes the hash and forces a write on every call. Fix is to move it below the boundary or remove it.

prompt: Our agent has a 2k-token tool definition list and a 4k-token system prompt with few-shot examples. ~30 calls per minute on the hot path, same prefix. Should we use prompt caching, and how?
rubric_notes: Clear yes. Should name `cache_control: ephemeral` on tools + system, mention 5-min TTL covers the call rate, and recommend logging cache_read/cache_write tokens to verify.

prompt: We turned on prompt caching last week but I'm not sure if it's actually working. The Anthropic bill looks about the same as before. What now?
rubric_notes: Instrument first. Should demand cache_read_input_tokens / cache_creation_input_tokens from the usage object before any tuning. 'I think it's caching' is not a metric.

prompt: We added `cache_control: ephemeral` on every message in the conversation history to be safe. Costs went up. Why?
rubric_notes: Cache writes cost 125% on Anthropic; reads cost 10%. Caching things that change every turn pays the write premium with no read benefit. Should recommend caching only the stable prefix.

prompt: Our multi-tenant app personalises the system prompt with the user's name and role at the top. Each user has stable settings. Can we still cache?
rubric_notes: Per-user content above the boundary kills the shared-prefix cache. Should recommend either moving personalisation below the boundary or per-user cache scoping. Also flag privacy concern if any user data leaks into a shared cache.

prompt: We're on Anthropic ephemeral cache. After ~10 minutes of inactivity the next call is slow and expensive again. Is this a bug?
rubric_notes: 5-min ephemeral TTL. Not a bug. Should explain the TTL, suggest a keepalive ping pattern only if call rate justifies it, and mention 1-hour beta cache if the workload pattern needs it.

prompt: We sort our 30-tool list by relevance score before each call so the model picks the right tool faster. Latency improved but our cache savings didn't materialise. Thoughts?
rubric_notes: Tool order is part of the cache key. Reordering per call = 0% hit rate. Should recommend deterministic order; use the model's tool-choice or descriptions to bias selection instead.

prompt: The user turn is by far the largest part of our prompt (long pasted log files). Should we cache it?
rubric_notes: User turn is dynamic by definition; caching it pays write cost on every call. Cache only stable content above the user turn. If the log is reused across turns of one conversation, cache it at the message it first appears.

prompt: Building a batch pipeline that runs ~1M documents through Claude with the same 3k-token instructions prepended to each. Worth caching?
rubric_notes: Yes — classic batch case. Put `cache_control: ephemeral` on the instructions block, document last. But: in batch with rapid sequential calls hitting same prefix, expect high hit rate; check whether 5-min TTL covers the throughput pattern, or use 1-hour beta cache.

prompt: We're migrating from OpenAI to Anthropic. We were getting good cache savings on OpenAI automatically. Anthropic just works the same way, right?
rubric_notes: No. OpenAI auto-caches prompts ≥1024 tokens with no opt-in. Anthropic requires explicit `cache_control: ephemeral` breakpoints (up to 4). Should explain the difference and tell the user what to add to their existing call shape.

prompt: Our system prompt starts with `Date: {today}\n`. We need the model to know the date. How do we keep caching working?
rubric_notes: Don't put a daily-changing value above the boundary unless you want one warm-up per day. Options: move it below the cache boundary (into the user turn), or accept a daily cache rotation, or pin the date in the user turn.

prompt: Our system prompt is 800 tokens. Anthropic. Should we add a cache_control breakpoint on it?
rubric_notes: Anthropic minimum cacheable block is ~1024 tokens. 800 won't cache. Options: leave it uncached, or pad with stable content (tool list, examples) to cross the threshold *if* the volume justifies the engineering.

prompt: Our cache hit rate is sitting at 30%. We expected ~90%. Where do I look first?
rubric_notes: Should ask for the first ~200 tokens of the system prompt to look for hidden dynamic values (timestamps, request ids, A/B variants). Also verify whether the user is actually counting reads vs writes correctly; 30% might be a measurement artefact.

prompt: Product wants to A/B test 4 system-prompt variants. Will prompt caching still work?
rubric_notes: Yes but each variant gets its own cache key, so warm-up cost is 4× and steady-state hit rate divides by variant share. Mitigations: pin the variant id below the boundary if the variant body is identical, or accept the cost during the test window.

prompt: We added a request id to the top of every system prompt for tracing. Cache stopped hitting entirely. Should we just turn off tracing?
rubric_notes: False dichotomy. Move the request id below the cache boundary or into metadata/headers; tracing does not require the id to be in the model-visible prompt above the cache boundary.

prompt: Should we cache conversation history? It grows every turn — by turn 8 it's ~6k tokens.
rubric_notes: Yes — put `cache_control: ephemeral` on the last message of each completed turn. Each subsequent turn reads the prior turns from cache. Anthropic supports up to 4 breakpoints; rotate which message gets the marker.

prompt: Looking at our usage data, cache-write tokens are costing us more than what no-cache would have cost. Caching seems like a net loss. Should we turn it off?
rubric_notes: Caching only pays off when reads ≥ ~1.3× writes (Anthropic 10% read / 125% write). Net loss = either the prefix changes every call (writes dominate) or call volume is too low to amortise. Should diagnose which, not just disable.

prompt: User uploads a 50k-token PDF and asks ~10 follow-up questions about it in one session. We're on Anthropic. How should we set up caching?
rubric_notes: Ideal use case. Put the PDF in the first user message with `cache_control: ephemeral`. The 10 follow-ups read the 50k tokens from cache at 10%. Should also mention that the 5-min TTL fits an interactive Q&A session; if sessions can pause, consider 1-hour beta cache.

prompt: We're multi-tenant. System prompt is shared across all users, then user-specific data is appended at the end. Are we good for shared caching?
rubric_notes: Yes — this is the right layout. Shared prefix above the boundary caches once across tenants; user data below the boundary is fine. Cache breakpoint goes at the end of the shared prefix. Verify nothing tenant-specific has leaked above the boundary.

prompt: Gemini has implicit prefix caching and explicit cachedContent. We have a 200k-token reference doc we reuse across days. Which mechanism?
rubric_notes: Explicit `cachedContent`. Implicit is per-session prefix; explicit is named, long-lived (hours-to-days), with deterministic key. For a 200k-token doc reused across days, explicit is the only sane choice. Implicit will rebuild on every cold session.
